old_claim.note: Magnetic Tape Data Storage provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains from computers_early to magnetic_tape_data_storage, since early mainframe computers predate practical magnetic tape data-storage drives and predate this node’s 1951 first-known date.

source_shape.source_locator: Computer History Museum ENIAC timeline and related narrative.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: ENIAC is an early stored-program era computer built before the 1951 digital magnetic tape data-storage systems became established.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source establishes early-computer development chronology that does not require magnetic tape storage as a prerequisite for the existence of early computers.

ontology_before: The graph made magnetic tape storage a direct enabler of early computer systems.
ontology_after: Early computers now depend on electronics, mathematics, computation theory, and vacuum tubes, with magnetic tape removed as a downstream dependency.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: removed computers_early -> magnetic_tape_data_storage edge to resolve an incorrect chronology inversion.
would_reject_if: ENIAC-era sources were found to use magnetic tape as an essential prerequisite for 1946 early computers.
The edge is reintroduced again without temporal correction.
Computers_early scope is changed to a post-1951 tape-dependent lineage.
short_reason: Digital magnetic tape storage became practical after the first vacuum-tube mainframes, so the direction was reversed.
